title: Natural algorithms for flow problems
speaker: Nisheeth Vishnoi
abstract: In the last few years, there has been a significant interest in the computational abilities of Physarum Polycephalum (a slime mold). This drew from a remarkable experiment which showed that this organism can compute shortest paths in a maze. Subsequently, the workings of Physarum were mathematically modeled as a dynamical system and algorithms inspired by this model were proposed to solve several graph problems.

proof_stages.label: Physarum dynamics as flow algorithms
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates Physarum dynamics from slime-mold behavior, formulates shortest path and undirected/directed min-cost flow problems, and recalls prior convergence results and the unresolved issues of rates and positivity.

proof_stages.label: Pseudopolynomial convergence theorems
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines the relevant efficiency notion, introduces Euler-discretized Physarum dynamics, and states the main results: convergence bounds for undirected and directed min-cost flow plus global existence for the continuous directed system.

proof_stages.label: Undirected electrical-flow setup and invariants
proof_stages.summary: For the undirected discrete dynamics, the proof fixes an optimal flow, expresses each step through Laplacians, voltages, currents, and energy, then proves positivity, uniform upper bounds, cut lower bounds, and existence of an x-capacitated proxy flow at every time.

proof_stages.label: Cost, energy, and logarithmic barrier progress
proof_stages.summary: The proof compares the weighted conductance cost with the optimum and the electrical energy, then introduces a log barrier weighted by an optimal flow to show that either cost decreases or the barrier increases whenever the current cost is still above optimum.

proof_stages.label: Voltage bound and completion of the undirected proof
proof_stages.summary: The remaining step bounds all potential differences by sorting vertices by voltage and applying a cut argument, allowing a step size of order 1/(nC) and completing the convergence-to-optimal-cost bound without requiring uniqueness of the optimal flow.

proof_stages.label: Directed dynamics via preconditioning and exclusion barriers
proof_stages.summary: For directed min-cost flow, the proof adds high-cost demand-pair edges, uses an induction linking positivity, capacitated flows, and bounded voltages, interprets iterates as approaching a geometrically weighted average of electrical flows, and applies barriers against nonoptimal basic flows.

proof_stages.label: Global existence for continuous directed dynamics
proof_stages.summary: The continuous-time proof reduces global existence to preventing any conductance from reaching zero in finite time, then uses a logarithmic barrier and bounded energy/voltage estimates to rule out such boundary hitting.

proof_stages.label: Consequences and open algorithmic directions
proof_stages.summary: The talk concludes that Physarum-type dynamics can solve undirected and directed flow problems and highlights open questions about sharper bounds, whether directed preconditioning is intrinsic, and better discretizations for truly efficient algorithms.

examples.name: Slime mold solving a maze
examples.note: The maze experiment shows a slime mold retracting to the shortest route between two food sources, motivating the mathematical shortest-path/Physarum-dynamics model rather than proving any convergence theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker walks through the maze experiment over time, explaining how the slime mold reorganizes and leaves the shortest path between the food sources

examples.name: Two parallel edges with costs C and C-1
examples.note: This tiny directed graph shows that Physarum dynamics may take time proportional to the numerical magnitude C to distinguish nearly equal costs, motivating the pseudopolynomial convergence notion.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the two-edge graph as a concrete lower-bound instance, describing the Physarum trajectory and that its convergence time is proportional to C

passes.reason: A collapsed two-vertex parallel-edge network with a minimal unit cost gap, used to show convergence can scale like C rather than log C.
a minimal two-parallel-edge near-tie with integer cost gap 1 is used to refute convergence polynomial in the bitlength of C
the minimal two-parallel-edge near-tie instance is a critical slow-convergence case used against input-size-polynomial convergence.
a minimal two-edge near-tie instance showing Physarum convergence cannot be polynomial in log C
a minimal two-parallel-edge network with an almost tied cost gap is used to show convergence need not be logarithmic in C

the two-edge near-tie instance is used to refute logarithmic-in-C convergence and is a minimal boundary bad case.

the two-vertex two-parallel-edge instance with costs C and C-1 is used to refute logarithmic-in-C efficient convergence, and is a minimal near-tie instance with the smallest nonzero cost gap
